key_departures.impact: Four-time Pro Bowl pass rusher with 57 sacks in his first four Bengals seasons. Missed 10 games in 2025 due to injury amid a prolonged contract dispute; Bengals declined to franchise tag him. Leaves a massive void as the team's most productive pass rusher of the past half-decade. Signed with division rival Baltimore on a 4yr/$112M deal — a direct AFC North upgrade for the Ravens and a direct downgrade for Cincinnati.

Back-to-back 5-sack seasons as a key rotational/situational starter. Logged a career-high 616 defensive snaps in 2025. His departure alongside Hendrickson leaves the Bengals' EDGE room in a state of near-complete rebuild. Myles Murphy (year 3) and Shemar Stewart (year 2) are the only returning options with experience.

Served as the starting TE. Modest production — his below-market exit contract reflects a role player more than an impact weapon. Saints deal at $4.375M AAV confirms he was replaceable. Still, there is now no clear No. 1 TE on the roster.

Bengals chose not to tender Browning as a restricted free agent. He started 10 games over three seasons as the emergency backup behind Burrow. Leaves the team thin behind Joe Burrow with zero proven backup QB on roster.
Depth/swing guard option. Low-value departure; Risner re-signing and Fairchild development offset this. Minimal need impact.

Productive starter who finished 2025 with a career-high 104 tackles and 13 interceptions over the last three seasons. Bengals signed Bryan Cook as a direct replacement, so the hole is filled. Departure is offset.
Started at CB2 for Cincinnati. His departure creates a genuine need at cornerback opposite DJ Turner II. Bengals have not signed a replacement yet.
Depth CB departure. Jalen Davis re-signed into the slot/nickel role. Low impact on CB need picture.
Veteran IOL depth. Departure does not escalate a need given Risner re-sign and Fairchild development.

signings.analysis: Three-year deal at $20M AAV is solidly in starter range for EDGE. Mafe is a Super Bowl champion fresh off the Seahawks' title run and ranked 8th among EDGE in pass rush win rate in 2025 despite just 2 sacks in 340 snaps. The Bengals are betting on win-rate-to-sacks conversion. Guarantee structure ($19M fully paid as Year 1 roster bonus) means he is fully committed as a starter in 2026 but the back years carry no guaranteed money — a partial commitment signal. He partially replaces Hendrickson's role but is not a Hendrickson-caliber player. Combined with Myles Murphy and Shemar Stewart, EDGE need is reduced but not eliminated. The team still lacks a proven elite pass rusher and will almost certainly target EDGE in the draft.

Three-year deal at $13.4M AAV is firmly in the starter tier for safety. Cook ranked 4th in overall PFF grade among safeties and 6th in coverage grade in 2025 for Kansas City's Super Bowl defense. The $14M fully guaranteed as a signing bonus establishes real commitment. Cook is the direct replacement for Geno Stone and pairs with Jordan Battle. Bengals' safety need is addressed — this is a clear upgrade over Stone. S drops from a need to a filled position.

Two-year deal at $13M AAV slots in at the mid-starter range for IDL. Allen is a two-time Pro Bowl selection and experienced interior disruptor, though he posted declining numbers in recent seasons (3.5 sacks, 32nd percentile pass rush win rate in 2025 with Minnesota). The $8M guarantee structure — effectively a 1-year commitment — signals this is a bridge/prove-it situation. Allen adds legitimate pass-rush ability from the interior and experienced leadership to a DL room (BJ Hill, TJ Slaton, Kris Jenkins Jr.) that was below average in pass rush win rate. This partially addresses IDL need but the age/production curve means Cincinnati likely still wants a longer-term IDL answer via the draft.

signings.notes: Re-signing/extension. Brown was entering his final contract year; this extension runs through 2028.
signings.analysis: Two-year extension at $16M AAV is squarely mid-market for a starting OT. Brown has started all 45 games at LT since joining in 2023, providing consistency even if not elite play. The $23M in first-12-month cash and extension through 2028 signals a genuine multi-year commitment to keeping the offensive line stable. OT (LT specifically) is fully addressed — Burrow has his blindside protector locked up through at least 2028. Brown negotiated this deal himself without an agent.

signings.notes: Re-signing. Risner returned on March 2, before legal tampering opened. Can earn up to $5M with incentives.
signings.analysis: One-year deal at $3.25M base is below starter-market for IOL, but this is a veteran on a prove-it/continuity deal who started 11 games in 2025 and was lauded as a key piece of what became the best Bengals OL in the Burrow era. The short commitment signals the team is either planning to transition Fairchild into the starting role or evaluating both. IOL is partially addressed — Risner provides a reliable starter for 2026 but the long-term interior guard spot is still a draft target.

signings.notes: Re-signing/extension on Feb. 20. Davis was the primary slot/nickel CB in 2025 and had a career-high snap share.
signings.analysis: One-year depth deal. Davis is a nickel specialist who had a breakout 2025 in terms of snaps played. His re-signing preserves the slot role but at depth pricing — this does nothing to address the outside CB2 vacancy left by Cam Taylor-Britt. CB remains a real need, particularly on the outside.

signings.notes: Signed March 14. Third stint with the Bengals.
signings.analysis: Veteran minimum deal. Johnson is a career backup in his third stint with Cincinnati, signed purely as an insurance body behind Joe Burrow following Jake Browning's departure to Tampa Bay. At 39, this is a camp/emergency option — it does not solve the backup QB need. ESPN noted the Bengals 'still need one more QB as a potential backup.' QB depth remains a live need.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Bryan Cook (3yr/$40.25M, $14M gtd) is a high-quality starter coming off a top-5 PFF grade at safety in 2025. Pairs with Jordan Battle. This need is done — drop to luxury/depth tier only.

Orlando Brown Jr. extension (2yr/$32M through 2028) locks up LT. Brown starts every game and provides continuity. Amarius Mims is developing at RT. OT is no longer a meaningful draft need — drop to depth/developmental tier.

Mafe signing (3yr/$60M, $20M AAV) partially addresses the Hendrickson void. However, $19M in guarantees concentrated in Year 1 with no back-year money, combined with Mafe's low sack total (2 in 2025), means this is not a definitive solution. Myles Murphy and Shemar Stewart provide young depth. EDGE remains a high-priority draft need — team still lacks a proven top-end rusher. ESPN and analysts confirm pass rush is still the top need heading into the draft.

Jonathan Allen signing (2yr/$26M) brings an experienced interior disruptor, though his declining production (3.5 sacks, declining PFF grades) and limited guarantees ($8M) cap the upgrade. BJ Hill and TJ Slaton remain as the backbone. IDL drops one tier — the room is improved but not elite. A Day 2 or Day 3 IDL via the draft is still warranted.

Dalton Risner re-signed (1yr/$3.25M) maintains a starting guard for 2026 alongside developing Dylan Fairchild. However, the 1-year deal means this is a bridge solution, not a long-term fix. Center Ted Karras is under contract. IOL drops a tier but long-term guard remains a draft consideration in rounds 3-5.

Cam Taylor-Britt's departure creates a CB2 hole outside. Jalen Davis re-signed but only into the nickel/slot role at depth pricing. No new outside CB signed. Need unchanged at tier 2 — this is a genuine draft priority for Day 2.

Noah Fant departed for New Orleans (2yr/$8.75M). No TE replacement has been signed — the only roster TE of note is Tanner Hudson on a 1-year extension (camp/depth). The Bengals have Joe Burrow, Ja'Marr Chase, and Tee Higgins but no functional receiving TE. Escalated to tier 2 — should target a TE in rounds 2-4 of the draft.

Zero moves at linebacker. Top options (Leo Chenal, Kaden Elliss) signed elsewhere in the first wave. Barrett Carter and Demetrius Knight Jr. are the primary options but OC/DC is unhappy with Knight in an OLB role. LB remains a genuine need — analysts report DC Al Golden specifically wanted a LB added. Should target LB in rounds 2-4.

Jake Browning departed to Tampa Bay (not tendered as RFA). Josh Johnson signed on a veteran minimum deal — purely an emergency body. Cincinnati still needs a legitimate backup QB capable of stepping in for Burrow if needed. ESPN confirmed the Bengals 'still need one more QB.' Tier unchanged.

key_additions.headline: Super Bowl champion pass rusher arrives to anchor post-Hendrickson EDGE room at $20M AAV
Top-5 PFF-graded safety in 2025 returns to his hometown to upgrade Cincinnati's beleaguered secondary
Former two-time Pro Bowl DT adds interior pass rush and veteran leadership to a struggling defensive front
LT Brown locks himself in through 2028, securing Burrow's blindside on a team-friendly $16M AAV extension he negotiated himself
Fan-favorite veteran guard returns on a one-year deal to anchor an interior line that turned into one of Burrow's best

key_losses.headline: 4x Pro Bowl pass rusher ends contentious Bengals tenure, signs blockbuster deal with division-rival Ravens — a painful double blow
Back-to-back 5-sack rotational EDGE departs, compounding a pass-rush crisis that defines Cincinnati's 2026 draft board
Starting TE departs to New Orleans, leaving the Bengals without a proven receiving tight end alongside Chase and Higgins
Starting outside CB departs in free agency, opening a genuine hole at CB2 that the Bengals have yet to address
Bengals decline to tender Browning as RFA, leaving the team without a capable backup QB behind the injury-prone Burrow
